ORIGINALITY.justification: The paper proposes a notable and conceptually novel framing by reducing relation extraction to natural-language reading-comprehension questions and using schema querification to cheaply generate a very large training set (30M examples for $1,250), which is a clear new perspective on data collection and problem formulation. Extending a neural QA model (BiDAF) with answerability variables and demonstrating zero-shot extraction to unseen relations represent non-trivial, practically useful techniques beyond straightforward reuse of prior models. However, the core modeling builds on existing QA architectures and distant supervision, and zero-shot performance is reported as substantially lower, so the contribution is significant but not a radical departure from prior work.

CLARITY.justification: The summary indicates the paper is well organized (intro → method → experiments → conclusion) and clearly states its main contributions early (the QA reduction, cheap dataset creation, and BiDAF extension). Writing quality is described as good and related work coverage thorough, suggesting consistent notation and clear exposition of methodology and experiments (including hyperparameters). One minor caveat is that the summary does not mention the effectiveness of figures/tables or whether all presentation details (e.g., example clarity) are fully polished, hence a 4 rather than a 5.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The main claims (recasting relation extraction as QA, creating a very large cheaply-labeled dataset, and adapting BiDAF with answerability to perform slot-filling) are clearly stated and supported by experiments on the querified WikiReading-derived dataset and reported Precision/Recall/F1 results, including analysis of known limitations (annotation noise, span granularity, lower zero-shot accuracy). There are no mathematical derivations to check. However, the experimental methodology is weakened by the absence of reported baselines or statistical significance testing and reliance on distantly supervised data (which the authors note is noisy), leaving some uncertainty about how the approach compares to alternative methods. Overall the results justify the main direction, but the evaluation lacks controls that would more firmly support the claims.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: The summary indicates no experimental baselines were compared ("Baselines compared: none mentioned"), so the paper fails to provide meaningful empirical comparisons despite reporting precision/recall/F1. Because no baselines are evaluated, fairness of comparisons cannot be assessed and the paper does not appear to acknowledge when prior methods might be comparable or better. On the positive side, the related-work coverage is described as thorough, suggesting relevant approaches are cited even if not empirically compared.

SUBSTANCE.justification: The paper evaluates large-scale training (30M examples) and tests generalization to unseen entities, unseen question templates, and zero-shot unseen relations, and reports error analyses (annotation noise 36%, span-granularity 42%) and limitations (answerability, lower zero-shot accuracy). However, no baselines are mentioned and ablation/sensitivity analyses are not reported in the summary, leaving gaps in assessing component contributions. Related work coverage is described as thorough, and hyperparameters are reported, but code/dataset release is not mentioned, so reproducibility is only partially supported.

IMPACT.justification: Recasting relation extraction as reading-comprehension questions is an important reframing that lets practitioners reuse strong neural QA models (extended BiDAF) and supports zero-shot slot-filling when relations are specified only at test time. The demonstrated ability to generate extremely large training sets cheaply via schema querification and distant supervision (30M examples for $1,250) materially lowers the barrier to large-scale IE training and is likely to be adopted and extended. While zero-shot accuracy to completely unseen relations is limited, the approach clearly opens significant new research directions (question-template design, answerability modeling, noise-robust training) and has potential impact beyond the narrow subfield of relation extraction.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: This work sits squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues: it addresses core problems in relation extraction and reading comprehension and proposes a novel cross-task formulation that is of strong interest to the community. The methodology (extending a neural QA model, large-scale dataset construction via schema querification and distant supervision) and the use of standard data (WikiReading) and metrics (Precision/Recall/F1) match expectations for such venues. The paper is aimed at the right audience (NLP/ML researchers interested in information extraction, QA, and zero-shot generalization) and is positioned with thorough related-work coverage and appropriate experimental reporting (hyperparameters).

main_claims: Relation extraction can be reduced to answering natural-language reading-comprehension questions by associating one or more questions with each relation slot, enabling relation extraction via neural QA models.
This reduction allows building very large training sets cheaply via schema querification combined with distant supervision (they generated 30,000,000 examples with a budget of $1,250).
A recent neural reading-comprehension model (BiDAF) can be extended with variables to model answerability and trained on the new dataset to perform slot-filling.

Zero-shot extraction to unseen relations (specified only at test time) is possible, and the approach generalizes well to unseen entities and unseen question templates, though zero-shot to unseen relations yields lower accuracy.
_summary.methodology: Reduce relation extraction to reading comprehension by pairing each relation slot with natural-language question(s) and train an extended BiDAF reading-comprehension model (with variables to model answerability) on large-scale data created via schema querification and distant supervision.
datasets: Querified slot-filling dataset derived from WikiReading (30,000,000 generated examples)
limitations: Zero-shot generalization to unseen relations is substantially more challenging and yields lower accuracy than for known relations/questions.
A nontrivial portion of errors are due to annotation noise in Wikidata (36% in a 50-example analysis).
A substantial portion of errors arise from inaccurate span selection/granularity (42% in the cited analysis), for which the model is fully penalized.
The method must also predict when a question is not answerable (i.e., model answerability).
